speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is November 29, 1996, interviewing Bernard Presser. My name is Shauna Sherker. We're in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia, and the interview will be conducted in English. November 29, 1996, Shauna Sherker interviewing Bernard Presser in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia. Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernard Presser, born the 16th of September, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go by any other names, Bernard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't have a nickname for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Berek was a nickname,

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a large family, Berek?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a brother and a sister. Sister is still living in Melbourne, and brother pass away in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yanek-- Yanek and his-- [INAUDIBLE] it was-- I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. If it comes back to you later, you can tell me then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonas.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bronislawa.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what are the name of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frania was my mother, and Chaim was my father name. He-- they call him [? Heinrich ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did your parents do to make a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was professional waiter. He own-- before the war, a [? big ?] big business. But he was [? cross with ?] his [? partner ?] [INAUDIBLE] business, he went away. And after the war, he was in, first of all, called to the military. But he came back. In between, I remember that my mother was attending hospital in school, where they [INAUDIBLE] for the hospital. And they-- she brought some [? programs. ?] I was about five or six. I still remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was not a nurse. She was [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did your family have a business, later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, later my mother was in a business for about 20 years. They call it Moulin Rouge. It was nightclub. And-- but was not very successful, in-- in 1930s, '35. He went out, penniless, and started a-- a small bistro. He bought it [INAUDIBLE]. And unfortunately, he passed away a few years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did he pass away?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the cause of his death?

speaker: interviewee
text: Heart-- he just collapsed behind a-- behind the counter. He was-- was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What happened to the business after your father died?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother run the business. I was-- my-- my brother got married, and after that I opened the business by 7 o'clock, stay until quarter to 9:00. I had to go to my job at 9 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Before you started working, were you a student?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because I was-- I finish [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] business college two years early. It was four years, but I-- I finished only two years of that. And I waited and went to work for, in the [INAUDIBLE], it was a kind of hardware. But all the tools and everything was-- was [INAUDIBLE] before.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what were you studying in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, that's the 11 subject of all-- was math, accountantcy, bookkeeping, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] French, German, stenography, geography.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your best subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I just couldn't-- the Polish, as well. Literature [? and so on. ?] But my best subject was physics.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what did you want to be when you were younger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I have plenty of idea. But I didn't realize that I can do it. I want to-- to-- because my family-- or rather, my mother's family-- ran a plumber [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] business. She was, as I remember, she was a widow [INAUDIBLE] 1940-- '21. She ran [INAUDIBLE] the business. I like-- like that one. I was even helping her, sometimes-- running on the [? roofs. ?] [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what you wanted to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just-- just-- as a kids like to be, say, policeman or some other things, you know, there's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of children in your neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends, growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have quite a few of them. After the-- when I was in sixth grade, I met my friends with whom I was very close to, for years and years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their name? [? Heinrich ?] [? Novak-- ?] his name was [? Friedman. ?] He changed to [? Novak. ?] [? Heinrich ?] [? Freidigger, ?] and [? Yusef ?] [? Longsberger, ?] and, well, another one, very-- very-- who-- I just can't remember. Maybe I remember it later. OK. And did they survive the war, your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only two of them, Novak and [? Freidigger. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you have any non-Jewish friends, growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very good-- good-- in contact with a non-Jewish friend. But it was not friend, rather colleagues. You know? They-- they-- I invited them to-- to my house. They invited me over. We had been playing football-- soccer, rather-- together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we-- we are going-- going very well [? with them, ?] [INAUDIBLE]. I remember in high school, when I was, there was-- on the first day of open the school, the University [INAUDIBLE], I was-- I was going with a-- two or three of the Polish colleagues [? when I was ?] [INAUDIBLE]. And they tell me, sit, stay in the-- in the middle of us. Nobody will touch us. We have been [INAUDIBLE] university. Was very-- every year it was the same story. What would happen to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they would beat me up. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would they beat you up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], he's a Jew, so that in the university, they have to change the place where I'm sitting, there's a Jewish section and a Polish section.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how did the teachers respond to the anti-semitism in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't take [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] notice, or just go over this [INAUDIBLE] later. They take them two, three weeks, and they [? think it ?] was quiet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you-- where did you live in Krakow, what address?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I remember two-- one in [NON-ENGLISH], this Jewish section of Krakow. And later, I was living in [NON-ENGLISH]. That was very close to my wife. But that was by coincidence. But there was another, not populated with Jew, not much, although maybe [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. [GERMAN], in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was your family very religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would rather say no. My father was observant, but I don't think that fanatic was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was going only in high holidays. The father was-- was taking us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the synagogue to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very small-- two rooms, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] opposite a very big synagogue, the high synagogue, we call it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was plenty of-- plenty of close friends, want to be in the shul, but it was-- it was in different surrounding, I believe. Ambition to be a chairman or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a rabbi in your shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And was your mother observant, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, well, to say the truth, she was not. Was this a source of conflict between your parents? No, there was not a conflict. My father accepted that one. And my mother was coming to shul on the High Holy days. But she was not observant. Besides, that wasn't-- we-- we have a kosher house-- not, maybe, to the extent what they are doing now. But it was a kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a bar mitzvah, but only very-- very small, not-- not with a-- bar mitzvah [INAUDIBLE] now, now they have what's [? according to the ?] Torah. And [? when ?] a very small kiddush, there was-- there's entertainment for-- was very simple. You have a cake and wine, or something the same as [INAUDIBLE]. That was everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You mentioned your wife. What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rose-Dora.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was very nice. I want to find-- we have been meeting, passing. I was going from my-- you know, in Poland we have the main, in the mid-day, the supper what is here. When I was going home from-- from home to the office, about 3 o'clock, she was coming from the school. And I know her. I met-- met his-- her brother, the oldest brother, in-- he was instructor-- ski instructor. And I met-- met him, and we be close-- close friends. But when I ask him to introduce me by his sister, she-- she was-- he was thinking that I meant his step-sister, and she was [INAUDIBLE]. A lot of my friends who-- who know her to introduce me to her. She-- they didn't want me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once I would bring my cousin-- maybe second cousin, but not exactly cousin, named [NON-ENGLISH], to the dance [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And the place was very popular for young people, and she was over there. And I told him, you go home [INAUDIBLE], because I have [INAUDIBLE] just [? clop ?] my friend's [INAUDIBLE], and I had the dance. And so that started. And when was that? When did you meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was 1936, I believe-- '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did your family like her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very much. And did her family like you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did her family like you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I know the-- the three brother of her, and they were very friendly with me. I was [INAUDIBLE] in the same business, which she was-- he was making for Alexander, who is now living in Sydney. He was making the same [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] business, what I was making, but in [INAUDIBLE] business. So we know each other before I know [? Ruja. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 22nd of October, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe the wedding to me? Was it a Jewish wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing. Rose-- [INAUDIBLE] my-- she had to push me to be married, because her brother went away when the war started, on a motorbike, to the east. And she was by herself, and she told me it's not-- not good too-- too-- that I am by myself and not married. So we decided to go have a Jewish wedding. There was just-- in [NON-ENGLISH], there was [? Kahal. ?] There was-- you know what is [? Kahal ?] is? And rabbi, he was a-- the wedding recite something. But because my wife was not-- was orphan-- both parents was pass away-- so she has a trustee from the court. Unfortunately, the-- the trustee was the oldest brother, who was away. When she-- when we came to the court, the judge asked her whether that-- she is not [INAUDIBLE], or something of the kind, because he has some reservation because the trustee was in, was [INAUDIBLE] give her a [? go. ?] She-- by-- by chance, she find a partner of her sister-in-law in the court, and he was representing her.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was the name of the rabbi who married you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You mentioned earlier that you would open up your family shop in the morning, and then go to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a clerk-- clerk in-- in [INAUDIBLE] '37. And later I went to the-- to-- to make-- my-- my duty as a-- in the military-- Polish military. And when I come back, I get proposition to be presented from this other place. [INAUDIBLE] Krakow district, that's all. So to-- I was, rather, very free-- wherever I want to go, I just have to come afternoon, give the report to the-- or whether I have some orders, or inquiries, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How long were you in the Polish army for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six-- 18 months. I was have a very-- and there was a-- a chap who was-- going with my cousin for years. And he was-- I believe he was a [INAUDIBLE]. It was the army who was fighting with the Australian-- Austrian people, against Russia. And he has a connection. I believe that he was doing that for money. And he told me-- usually, the-- the duty was 24 months. But when I-- and I have to go wherever they send me. And I ask whether they could arrange for me to-- to be in Krakow. And he told me, yes, and there is a one thing to have this-- then you'll be much better off, because you'll be only 18 months, or something of this kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was your assignment in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were-- what were your orders? What kind of orders?

speaker: interviewee
text: What job did you do, in the 18 months?

speaker: interviewer
text: 18 months, I was-- I don't know whether I call it-- just-- They want me to-- to take to the officer's school, but I don't want to, because I was not happy with it-- with all-- discipline and everything. I was hating it. What was your rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just-- I don't know how to call it. Just a soldier. I didn't even accepted the capital, what they want from me, because you have to be leader, and leader have to be rather following the orders for-- for-- for the [INAUDIBLE] and so on. No, I didn't agree with something, and I don't want to be the-- the leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated as a Jew in the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was treated rather good, but there was-- in '35, some kind of [INAUDIBLE]-- some type-- some [INAUDIBLE], and there was very bad going on, things. But one of the-- the-- they want to take [INAUDIBLE] will be, he was the commander of the 6th Division. He took command of the military. And there was a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was a written [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] what-- what soldier can do, what have to do, all that [? no. ?] Between that one was that Jewish people or people from the same state would-- would-- have to be the-- the army assistant can be excused from the duty.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where we been staying in-- in front of the door, I have to step out three fee-- three feet, or three steps. And this-- this [INAUDIBLE] who is-- who was the head of the company asked, what are you doing? I ask-- I tell him, I am from Krakow. I have to be excused. The next answer was, Jewish and people who are from Krakow, step out. What I [INAUDIBLE] that Jewish, even who was living in Krakow, have to step out. So when did you leave the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1937-- 1937 is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: '37 Can you describe to me what was happening in the Jewish community in Krakow at that time? Were you active in the Jewish community, at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: As in a small way. I was a member of-- of Jewish Zionist organization--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which?

speaker: interviewee
text: --not very active. But it's very attractive, because we have been going to excursions [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the organization?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Akiba. ?] And then later, when I was in high school, there was a-- another one, only I rather like [INAUDIBLE] the way to-- you know, by this time we change the books from the year, from-- from that higher class to the lower class. And they have been trying to help financially, because we have been doing so. And I couldn't remember that name. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. So what-- was there a leader in the Jewish community in Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of leader? What kind of leader?

speaker: interviewer
text: A community leader, an elder, somebody who--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was quite a few of them. I remember Dr. Ton, who was a rabbi, and a very progressive one. He was member of parliament, in Poland parliament.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. Ton.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: T-- T-O-N. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Ton. Were you reading any newspapers? Was there a Jewish newspaper?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Polish.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And can you tell me what happened on September 6, 1939, when the Germans occupied Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was first of-- it was the 1st of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was called to the army back in 15 of August. The staff in our company couldn't believe that the war would be. So they give us the rifles, only five ammunition. And I was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was sent to the patrol, to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] main station in Krakow, to-- to direct the people who were coming [INAUDIBLE], coming to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know how it is. [INAUDIBLE] something or company, new companies, was [INAUDIBLE] company. [INAUDIBLE] and I was-- I have 20 people with me. And I said one, to take him away, but then he is coming back. That was taking a few days-- rather, two days or three days. On the 1st of September, I was in with another leader of patrol from second [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] air-- air-- air force in Krakow, in the trenches-- because was the sight of alarm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm gonna stop you here, because we have to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we come back, you can continue telling me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
